schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch set the pious Hezekiah's accession against Judah's Assyrian bondage and the fall of Samaria; Ellicott finds the king's whole history in his name, 'My strength is Jah.'
Matthew Henry marvels that so good a son sprang from so wicked a father, proof that goodness is not of nature but of sovereign grace.
Gill dates the notice to the third year of Hoshea's revolt from Assyria.
Clarke surveys the whole chapter in advance and refers the chronology to his earlier discussion.

themes: the meaning of the name Hezekiah

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch pass to the verdict that Hezekiah walked after David; Ellicott would read the mother's name 'Abi' as 'Abijah,' with the Chronicler.
Gill eases the chronology with Grotius, by the rounding of the kings' incomplete years.
Wesley resolves the same numbers by a co-regency, reckoning Ahaz's years from his sole reign.

debates.question: How can the regnal numbers stand, when Ahaz would have been only about eleven at Hezekiah's birth?

sides.position: Round the incomplete regnal years, with Grotius, widening the age-gap
Reckon Ahaz's sixteen years from his sole reign, allowing a co-regency

schools.summary: Gill weighs 'according to all that David did' as a distinction: Hezekiah matched David where lesser kings only did right.
Clarke fills the verdict with the cleansing of the temple, the restored Passover, and the general reformation of 2 Chronicles 29.
Keil and Delitzsch summarize the reign as walking in David's footsteps, removing idolatry and cleaving to Jehovah.
Keil and Delitzsch gather the idols under their Hebrew names; Ellicott defends the Mosaic antiquity of the serpent and weighs the etymology of Nehushtan.
Clarke examines the name and its abuse as an amulet; Wesley calls the serpent now nothing but brass, and notes not all high places were removed.
Matthew Henry: a God-given thing, once idolized, is better parted with than kept; the serpent becomes a type of Christ in John 3:14.
Gill marks the boldness of removing the high places, which the best earlier kings never dared.

sides.position: A term of contempt, merely 'a piece of brass'
Possibly 'the serpent' or 'great serpent,' though popularly derived from 'brass'

outliers.note: Digresses from Nehushtan into arguing that the nachash of Genesis 3 was no serpent but a creature of the ape genus, and mocks the Milan relic that claims to be the very serpent Moses raised.

themes: the serpent a type of Christ

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch reconcile 'none like him' with the same said of Josiah: Hezekiah pre-eminent in trust, Josiah in keeping the law.
Gill: he trusted the Lord, not idols as an arm of flesh; the Targum makes it trust in the Word of the Lord, whom the serpent typified.
Clarke draws the king's character as a chain of trust and obedience; Wesley notes he sought no foreign succours, unlike Ahaz and Asa.

themes: none like him among Judah's kings

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch weigh the verb dabak, 'he clave,' as meaning he never gave himself to idolatry; Ellicott parses the clauses as pious feeling joined to obedient practice.
Gill glosses the cleaving as adherence to the Lord's worship and the fear of the Lord.

themes: kept the commandments given to Moses

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch tie the prospering to the cleaving and date the revolt after Shalmaneser; Ellicott reads vv.1-12 as a preliminary sketch, the revolt resumed at v.13.
Gill: the Word of the Lord was for his help.
Wesley defends the revolt as righteous, contrasting it with Zedekiah's oath-breaking, since God owned and never reproved it.
Keil and Ellicott take 'from the watchtower to the fenced city' of all the towns, and place the Philistine campaign probably after Sennacherib's defeat.
Gill: Hezekiah recovered the towns the Philistines had seized under Ahaz and drove them to Gaza.
Clarke reads 'from the tower of the watchmen' as a proverb: he reduced every fortification, nothing standing before him.
Keil and Delitzsch explain the fall of Samaria is retold from Judah's annals as an event memorable to the whole covenant-nation.
Matthew Henry reads the repetition as spur, lament, and warning: a neighbour's house on fire while one's own is in danger.
Gill dates the siege to the opening of Hezekiah's fourth year.
Clarke refers the history back to 2 Kings 17:3.
Ellicott argues 'they took it' means the Assyrians (Sargon, not Shalmaneser); Keil and Delitzsch note the parallel account in Isaiah 36-39 and 2 Chronicles 32.
Gill reads 'at the end of three years' as at their beginning, and dates the fall to Hezekiah's sixth year.

debates.question: How is 'they took it' to be read, and who took Samaria?

sides.position: Plural: the Assyrians took it, and it was Sargon, not Shalmaneser

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch bind the deportation to its cause in v.12: Israel carried away because they broke the covenant.
Gill notes only the places of dispersion, treated where the removal was first recorded.
Matthew Henry reads the captivity as a calamity Hezekiah lamented but could not prevent, and a warning to Judah.

themes: Halah, Habor, Gozan, and the cities of the Medes

schools.summary: Ellicott calls this remark the 'theme' of the historical abstract, suggesting the reflections of 2 Kings 17:7-23, and reads 'all that Moses commanded' as apposition to the covenant.
Gill: they obeyed not the voice of the Lord in his law and by his prophets.
Matthew Henry seizes on the doubled refusal: they would neither hear nor do, resolved not to obey and so careless even to hear.
Keil and Delitzsch open the main narrative and point to the parallels; Ellicott identifies Sin-ahi-erib and presses the date.
Wesley reads the success granted Sennacherib as given to lift him to a more shameful ruin and to occasion the miraculous deliverance.
Matthew Henry weighs how much Hezekiah lost, and reads the invasion as a trial of faith and a chastening of a half-hearted people.
Gill dates it eight years after the captivity of Israel.

outliers.note: Presses the divergence between the Bible's fourteenth year (c. 714 B.C.) and Sennacherib's own monuments (701 B.C.), insisting it must not be explained away, since the Assyrian records are strictly contemporary.

themes: Sennacherib comes against the fenced cities
success given to lift the proud to ruin

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch report the fortifying of Jerusalem and the confession 'I have sinned' as a vassal's; Ellicott gives the topography of Lachish and reconciles the tribute figures.
Gill reads the embassy as weakness and distrust of God's providence, after Hezekiah first resolved to defend himself.
Matthew Henry cries, Where was Hezekiah's courage and confidence in God, that he did not first advise with Isaiah?
Wesley notes the three hundred talents came to above two hundred thousand pounds.

debates.question: Was Hezekiah's payment of tribute a sinful failure of faith?

sides.position: Yes - weakness and distrust of the power and providence of God
Reported without blame, as a prudent vassal's submission

outliers.note: Harmonizes the three hundred talents of silver here with the eight hundred of Sennacherib's own record by the lighter Babylonian talent standard.

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch show the tribute bought nothing, for Sennacherib took the money yet sent his generals against Jerusalem; Ellicott glosses 'the silver' as the money.
Gill: to make up the tribute the king exhausted both temple and palace treasuries, as kings of Judah had done before in distress.
Matthew Henry faults the fear that emptied the treasuries, yet likens it to David's eating of the shewbread in necessity.

themes: emptying the temple and royal treasuries
the resource of kings in distress

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'cut off' as 'stripped,' the word used of Ahaz in 2 Kings 16:17, and takes the 'pillars' as the framework of the gold-plated doors.
Gill: the plates of gold covering the doors were scraped off, as the Targum interprets.
Matthew Henry sets the stripping beside David's shewbread: a part given, in urgent need, for the preservation of the whole.

themes: stripping the gold from the temple doors
the doors and pillars Hezekiah had overlaid
the reformer undoing his own work

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies Tartan as the Assyrian tur-ta-nu (commander-in-chief) and locates the conduit of Isaiah 7:3; Keil and Delitzsch stress the perfidy of sending the host after taking the money.
Clarke reads the three names as offices, not persons, and glosses each; Wesley says the broken faith justified the revolt and prepared Sennacherib's ruin.
Gill notes the message is recorded almost word for word in Isaiah 36, Rabshakeh being the chief speaker.
Matthew Henry brands Sennacherib base and perfidious, and commends Hezekiah's prudence in declining a personal parley.

outliers.note: Draws a modern parallel between Rabshakeh's blasphemous manifesto and the Duke of Brunswick's 1792 manifesto in the French Revolution, both followed by disaster.

themes: Tartan, Rabsaris, Rabshakeh - offices not names
the great host at the conduit of the upper pool

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch name the three ministers and their offices (household, scribe, recorder) and summarize the coming speech; Ellicott compares the officer-lists of 1 Kings 4.
Clarke suspects the summons aimed to seize the king's person, and renders the offices in the terms of his own day.
Matthew Henry: Rabshakeh demanded the king, but Hezekiah prudently sent his ministers instead.

themes: the king declines a personal parley
the offices of household, scribe, and recorder

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch explain 'the great king' as the standing imperial title; Ellicott enlarges the grandiloquence from the inscriptions and the 'king of princes' of Hosea 8:10.
Clarke takes bittachon as a diminutive of contempt for Hezekiah's God; Wesley asks what the greatest men are when God contends with them.
Matthew Henry: Rabshakeh makes an idol of his master, but to faith the King of kings makes even Assyria look little.

outliers.note: Takes the Hebrew bittachon as a diminutive, a deliberate belittling of Hezekiah's God.

themes: 'the great king, the king of Assyria'
little gods and the King of kings

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch render 'counsel and strength' a mere word of the lips, not of the heart (Prov. 14:23); Ellicott reads it as insincere lip-service.
Matthew Henry answers the sneer with the fact: Hezekiah really had counsel and strength, which Rabshakeh mocks as vain words.

themes: a mere word of the lips

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch unfold the reed that pierces the hand (Ezek. 29:6-7) and recall Isaiah's warning against Egypt; Ellicott prefers 'cracked' and gathers the prophet's denunciations.
Clarke notes Egypt was already bruised and broken by its wars with Assyria.
Matthew Henry turns the reed to a sermon: every arm of flesh fails and wounds, but God is the rock of ages.

themes: the reed that pierces the hand
arm of flesh versus the rock of ages

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch show Rabshakeh, from a heathen view, casting the reform as an affront to Jehovah; Ellicott notes it is the natural construction a heathen would put on the abolition of the high places.
Clarke calls the argument artfully malicious; Wesley marvels that he judges the great God by petty gods, measuring piety by many altars.
Matthew Henry: one of the king's best deeds is here misconstrued as impious by one ignorant of God's law.

themes: the taunt about the high places
a heathen reading of the reform

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Judah could not find riders for two thousand horses; Ellicott surveys the disputed opening verb and the sneer at Judah's want of cavalry.
Clarke feels the insult: couldst thou even find riders, how then stand against the smallest division?
Matthew Henry: with utmost haughtiness Rabshakeh insinuates Hezekiah has no soldiers fit for the field.

themes: 'give pledges' - a wager or a compact?

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch make it the conclusion of the horse-taunt; Ellicott takes 'one captain of the least' as the pasha meaning himself, and stops before the reproach about Egypt.
Matthew Henry weaves the two threads together: Judah's supposed weakness and her supposed false ally in Egypt.

themes: the least of the king's servants

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch allow the prophets' predictions may have reached the Assyrian; Ellicott surveys the theories and cites the parallels of Nabonidus and the Moabite Stone, with Isaiah 10:5.
Wesley calls the claim a forgery to strike terror; Clarke reads it as a bid to persuade Judah that Jehovah had become the Assyrian's ally.
Matthew Henry: all banter and bravado, though the enemy may have taken colour from the prophets' word on Samaria's fall.

debates.question: Had Rabshakeh any real basis for his claim that the LORD sent him?

sides.position: Pure invention, forged to strike terror
Perhaps grounded in captive northern priests or the Hebrew prophets' menaces
A mere inference from his run of success

themes: 'Am I come up without the LORD?'
the claim of a divine commission
the boast truer than he knew

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch explain the request for Aramaic to shield the people on the wall; Ellicott adds the philology of Aramaic as the tongue of diplomacy and 'the Jews' language.'
Clarke: the blasphemer's aim was to stir the people to sedition and deliver up the city.
Matthew Henry judges the request reasonable, but sees it only exasperated an unreasonable man.
Keil and Delitzsch note Rabshakeh turns to the men on the wall and the Masoretic euphemisms; Ellicott reads the coarse words as the famine-horror of siege, as in 2 Kings 6:25.
Clarke: the men are warned that resistance means eating their own excrement; Wesley, that he tells them to what miseries he will force them.

themes: aimed at the men on the wall

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: he stationed himself for effect and cried aloud that neither Hezekiah nor Jehovah could deliver; Ellicott notes he came nearer the wall.
Clarke: all to make the people revolt; Wesley reports the tradition that Rabshakeh was an apostate Jew, whose enmity was the more bitter.
Matthew Henry hears 'that great king' pressed again, and repeats the tradition of the apostate, comparing Julian.

themes: the tradition of an apostate Jew

schools.summary: Ellicott shows Rabshakeh posing as the people's friend, the verb 'deceive' being that of Genesis 3:13, and corrects 'his hand' to 'my hand'; Keil and Delitzsch read the wedge driven between people and throne.
Matthew Henry hears the hard alternative, bend or break, and turns it upon the sinner's peace with God.
Ellicott sets the two thrusts side by side (Hezekiah cannot, Jehovah will not) and disputes the notion of a late 'Jewish colouring'; Keil and Delitzsch read the assault moving to faith itself.
Matthew Henry: this was the enemy's chief endeavour, to shake the confidence in God that was the people's great stay, as David's enemies and Christ's mockers did.

themes: do not let him make you trust in the LORD
the God whose sanctuary is on Zion

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch render 'make a blessing' as make peace and 'come out' as surrender, the vine and cistern a figure of security; Ellicott ties the phrase to the goodwill of peace-making and the Egyptian campaign.
Wesley catches the honeyed terms: on these conditions I will give you no disturbance.
Matthew Henry marvels at the effrontery of offering an agreement by present, when the last such agreement was so grossly broken; gild the chain and the vulgar submit.

themes: every man his vine and fig tree

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch place the fair land as the doorway to deportation; Ellicott glosses 'oil olive' as the cultivated tree and 'that ye may live' as a promise of immunity.
Clarke sees the sedition: yield, and Sennacherib will transport you to a land as good as your own.
Matthew Henry strips the promise bare: a captivity dressed as a kindness, the devil painting the prison a paradise.

themes: the promise of a fair captivity

schools.summary: Ellicott admits a verbal inconsistency in an extemporised address and takes 'out of the hand of Assyria' of the predecessors too; Keil and Delitzsch put the bald inference that Jehovah can no more save than Hezekiah.
Matthew Henry lays open the pride and profaneness: he ranks the only living and true God with the upstart gods of the heathen.
Ellicott supplies the record of Sargon's conquest of Hamath and Arpad and weighs the obscure Hena and Ivah; Keil and Delitzsch join the verse to its blasphemous conclusion.
Clarke calls the blasphemy without parallel: Sennacherib boasts himself greater than any god of the nations.
Matthew Henry: the God of Israel is no local deity to be ranked with the upstart gods of Hamath and Arpad.

sides.position: Genuine place-names, the Avva and Avites of 2 Kings 17:24
Hebrew verbs ('he made to wander and overturned') or an epithet of Sepharvaim

themes: where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad?

schools.summary: Ellicott notes Ewald's rendering 'much less will Jehovah deliver'; Keil and Delitzsch draw the inference that levels the God of Israel with the idols of the nations.
Clarke names the impudent blasphemy without parallel, which Hezekiah refers to Jehovah to punish.
Matthew Henry marks the profaneness: the God of the whole earth, the Ancient of Days, made no more of than the gods of Hamath.

themes: the God of Israel levelled with idols

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch explain the commanded silence: to stop Rabshakeh and leave the envoys uncertain of the effect; the deputies return with rent clothes.
Clarke reads it as committing the cause to God: the blasphemy insults Jehovah, not you; let him avenge his own quarrel.
Matthew Henry: a time to keep silence; to answer a blasphemer is to cast pearls before swine, and the matter is left with the Lord.
Keil and Delitzsch record the ministers returning with clothes rent, the sign of their grief, to report Rabshakeh's words.
Clarke explains the rending of clothes at blasphemy and the chain of mediation: ministers to king, king to prophet, prophet to God.
Matthew Henry: they rent their clothes in detestation of the blasphemy and grief for Jerusalem, and faithfully reported all to the king.

themes: the ministers return with rent clothes
the matter laid before the king
